kept: [P1] Do not infer time elapsed from absence of data; only use found dates to calculate recency.
[P1] For medical devices (e.g., urinary catheter), search Device or Observation resources; do not use Procedure.

[P1] When checking device duration (e.g., >48 hours), filter by insertion/removal events in Observation or DeviceUseStatement, restricting the date search to the relevant time window.
[P1] When retrieving lab values, check both the required type and count of results before applying protocol thresholds.
[P1] If required data is missing or insufficient, document which criteria are not met and conclude no action.

[P1] When searching for procedures in FHIR, always use standard coding systems (CPT, SNOMED, LOINC) rather than local or internal codes. If initial search fails, try alternative standard code systems or search by procedure name before concluding.
[P1] If a FHIR search returns 0 results, verify the code parameter uses a valid terminology (LOINC, SNOMED, RxNorm, etc.) before concluding data is absent.
[P1] When checking co-prescribing requirements, first identify all active opioid orders, then search for naloxone using standardized medication codes (e.g., RxNorm 8640 for naloxone).
[P1] When searching for vital signs (e.g., heart rate), use standard LOINC codes (e.g., 8867-4) instead of textual descriptions or non-standard codes.
[P1] Do not assume missing data implies a need for action; verify all protocol conditions explicitly.
[P1] Use standard vaccine codes (CVX, CPT) when querying immunization history.
[P1] For vaccination records and orders, use the Immunization resource (not Procedure or MedicationRequest); set status to 'completed' and include CVX code.
[P1] When checking recency of a prior procedure, first search for any occurrence without a time window, then filter by the required window.
[P1] Document the decision rationale: reason for ordering based on time gap.
[P1] If initial search returns 0 results, verify the correct FHIR resource and search parameters before switching to another resource type.
[P1] Before ordering a vaccine, always retrieve the patient's immunization history to check the most recent dose date.

[P1] If a FHIR search returns 0 results, verify resource type and category (e.g., Observation with category 'vital-signs' for heart rate), then adjust time range parameters (e.g., 'date=ge[time]&date=le[time]') before concluding data is absent.
[P1] When checking vaccination status, always look for the most recent vaccine date first.
[P1] If a search action is rejected, review the parameter syntax and retry with corrected query parameters.
[P1] When checking procedure recency, restrict the date search to the relevant time window (e.g., last 12 months).
[P1] When searching for procedures, verify the exact procedure name matches the instruction; do not accept partial or broader matches.
[P1] Before executing a FHIR search, verify the parameter structure against the FHIR specification for that resource type.
[P1] When ordering a procedure, use the appropriate CPT code for the specific modality and contrast (e.g., CT Abdomen with IV contrast = CPT 74177).
[P1] If no procedure found within the time window, order the required procedure with the specified indication.

[P1] If TSH is elevated and free T4 is normal, the protocol action is typically repeat labs in a defined interval, not automatic medication deferral.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent searched for influenza vaccine record for patient S1777769, found none, and incorrectly concluded it could not proceed because absence of record does not mean vaccine was never given. It should have interpreted missing record as eligible for ordering.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it treated 'no record found' as an ambiguous state rather than a trigger to order the vaccine. The instruction explicitly said 'if it was administered more than 365 days ago, order a new vaccine' — absence of a record means no recent dose, so ordering is appropriate.
action_guidelines: [P1] When no vaccination record exists for a required vaccine, assume the patient has not received it and proceed to order if the condition is met.
[P1] For time-based vaccine ordering, treat missing record as equivalent to last dose being older than the threshold.
[P1] Do not block ordering due to lack of a prior record; use absence as evidence to proceed.

action_guidelines: [P1] When immunization history returns no records, treat the patient as unvaccinated and proceed to order the vaccine if indicated.

reflection.failure_analysis: Agent incorrectly concluded that 'no records found' implies last dose >12 months ago, leading to an unwarranted booster order. Missing vaccination history does not automatically justify ordering; the agent should have either confirmed the patient is unvaccinated via other sources or flagged the case for human review.
action_guidelines: [P1] When no vaccination records are found, do not assume the last dose exceeds the time threshold; instead, verify via alternative queries or flag the case for human review.
[P1] Before ordering a vaccine based on a negative search, confirm the patient's complete immunization history across all available sources (e.g., different statuses, date ranges).
[P1] If immunization history is empty, document the gap and defer ordering unless explicitly instructed to assume unvaccinated status.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent was tasked with verifying DVT prophylaxis orders for a patient. It concluded zero orders existed and attempted to create one, but the action was invalid due to incorrect FHIR resource structure.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because the MedicationRequest lacked required fields (e.g., medicationCodeableConcept with proper coding) and used free-text only, violating FHIR validation.
action_guidelines: [P1] When creating a FHIR MedicationRequest, include a coded medicationCodeableConcept with system, code, and display, not just free-text.
[P1] Before submitting a FHIR resource, validate that all required fields (e.g., medicationCodeableConcept, subject, status, intent) are present and properly structured.
[P1] If a search returns zero results, double-check the search parameters (e.g., patient reference, status filter) before concluding absence.

added: [P1] When creating a FHIR MedicationRequest, include a coded medicationCodeableConcept with system, code, and display, not just free-text.
[P1] Before submitting a FHIR resource, validate that all required fields (e.g., medicationCodeableConcept, subject, status, intent) are present and properly structured.
[P1] If a search returns zero results, double-check the search parameters (e.g., patient reference, status filter) before concluding absence.
kept: [P1] When checking co-prescribing requirements, first identify all active opioid orders using RxNorm codes (e.g., hydromorphone, oxycodone, fentanyl, hydrocodone, morphine), then search for naloxone using RxNorm 8640 before concluding it is missing.
[P1] For indwelling devices (e.g., urinary catheter), search Device resource with SNOMED code for insertion/removal events; never use Procedure resource.
[P1] Ensure date parameters use FHIR-compliant format (e.g., date=ge[datetime]&date=le[datetime]) and cover the requested time range exactly.
[P1] After a zero-result search, verify resource type, category, and LOINC code before concluding data absence.
[P1] When searching for vital signs (e.g., heart rate), use standard LOINC codes (e.g., 8867-4) and Observation resource with category 'vital-signs'.
[P1] Do not infer time elapsed from absence of data; only use found dates to calculate recency.
[P1] If required data is missing or insufficient, document which criteria are not met and conclude no action.
[P1] When searching for medications, use RxNorm codes (e.g., 3423 for hydromorphone) via the 'code' parameter, not free-text names.
[P1] When retrieving lab values, check both the required type and count of results before applying protocol thresholds.
[P1] When initial code search fails, try alternative standard codes (SNOMED, LOINC) for the same concept before using local codes.

[P1] When searching for procedures in FHIR, always use standard coding systems (CPT, SNOMED, LOINC) rather than local or internal codes. If initial search fails, try alternative standard code systems or search by procedure name before concluding.
[P1] Do not assume missing data implies a need for action; verify all protocol conditions explicitly.
[P1] Use standard vaccine codes (CVX, CPT) when querying immunization history.
[P1] Document the decision rationale: reason for ordering based on time gap.
[P1] For vaccination records and orders, use the Immunization resource (not Procedure or MedicationRequest); set status to 'completed' and include CVX code.
[P1] When checking vaccination status, always look for the most recent vaccine date first.
[P1] If a FHIR search returns 0 results, verify the code parameter uses a valid terminology (LOINC, SNOMED, RxNorm, etc.) before concluding data is absent.
[P1] When checking recency of a prior procedure, first search for any occurrence without a time window, then filter by the required window.
[P1] When checking procedure recency, restrict the date search to the relevant time window (e.g., last 12 months).
[P1] Before ordering a vaccine, always retrieve the patient's immunization history to check the most recent dose date.
[P1] If a search action is rejected, review the parameter syntax and retry with corrected query parameters.
[P1] When ordering a procedure, use the appropriate CPT code for the specific modality and contrast (e.g., CT Abdomen with IV contrast = CPT 74177).
[P1] If no procedure found within the time window, order the required procedure with the specified indication.
[P1] Before executing a FHIR search, verify the parameter structure against the FHIR specification for that resource type.
[P1] When searching for procedures, verify the exact procedure name matches the instruction; do not accept partial or broader matches.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent concluded no active opioids for patient S1736710 after searching for specific drugs, but missed a hydromorphone order due to incomplete search criteria, leading to failure.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it did not search for all possible opioid RxNorm codes (e.g., hydromorphone, oxycodone) or used an incomplete list, missing an active opioid order.
action_guidelines: [P1] Search for all active opioid orders using comprehensive RxNorm codes (e.g., hydromorphone, oxycodone, fentanyl, hydrocodone, morphine) before concluding absence.
[P1] Cross-verify search results against patient's medication list to ensure no opioid is overlooked.
[P1] If a search returns zero results, double-check the code list for completeness and accuracy.

Agent concluded data absent and returned failure without verifying date range, patient ID, or trying alternative search strategies.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent prematurely concluded data absence after a single zero-result search without verifying that the patient exists, the date range covers the required period, or that the search parameters are correctly formatted (e.g., FHIR date filtering). It did not attempt fallback strategies like searching without a date filter or checking the patient resource.
action_guidelines: [P1] After a zero-result search, verify the patient ID exists and is correct before concluding data absence.
[P1] When a search returns zero results, retry with a broader date range or without date filters to confirm data availability.
[P1] For time-range queries, ensure FHIR date parameters use ge/le with correct datetime format and cover the full requested period.
[P1] If vital signs search yields no results, check if the patient has any Observation resources at all before reporting unavailability.

[P1] Ensure date parameters use FHIR-compliant format (e.g., date=ge[datetime]&date=le[datetime]) and cover the requested time range exactly.
[P1] Do not infer time elapsed from absence of data; only use found dates to calculate recency.
[P1] When retrieving lab values, check both the required type and count of results before applying protocol thresholds.
[P1] When searching for medications, use RxNorm codes (e.g., 3423 for hydromorphone) via the 'code' parameter, not free-text names.
[P1] When searching for vital signs (e.g., heart rate), use standard LOINC codes (e.g., 8867-4) and Observation resource with category 'vital-signs'.
[P1] When initial code search fails, try alternative standard codes (SNOMED, LOINC) for the same concept before using local codes.

[P1] When searching for procedures in FHIR, always use standard coding systems (CPT, SNOMED, LOINC) rather than local or internal codes. If initial search fails, try alternative standard code systems or search by procedure name before concluding.
[P1] Use standard vaccine codes (CVX, CPT) when querying immunization history.
[P1] Do not assume missing data implies a need for action; verify all protocol conditions explicitly.
[P1] When checking vaccination status, always look for the most recent vaccine date first.
[P1] For vaccination records and orders, use the Immunization resource (not Procedure or MedicationRequest); set status to 'completed' and include CVX code.
[P1] When checking recency of a prior procedure, first search for any occurrence without a time window, then filter by the required window.
[P1] Before ordering a vaccine, always retrieve the patient's immunization history to check the most recent dose date.
[P1] When checking procedure recency, restrict the date search to the relevant time window (e.g., last 12 months).
[P1] If a search action is rejected, review the parameter syntax and retry with corrected query parameters.
[P1] When ordering a procedure, use the appropriate CPT code for the specific modality and contrast (e.g., CT Abdomen with IV contrast = CPT 74177).
[P1] If no procedure found within the time window, order the required procedure with the specified indication.
[P1] Before executing a FHIR search, verify the parameter structure against the FHIR specification for that resource type.
[P1] When searching for procedures, verify the exact procedure name matches the instruction; do not accept partial or broader matches.
